title: Quadlet volume not recreated after being removed
body:
### Issue Description

When using `.volume` files, these produce services with `RemainAfterExit=Yes`. This means a volume is created only once and when it is subsequently deleted (which I did because I had updated the volume options in the .volume file), it is not recreated by the quadlet service files (and instead created by podman run implicitly with default options).


### Steps to reproduce the issue

```
❯ sudo tee /etc/containers/systemd/foo.container > /dev/null << EOF
[Container]
Image=docker.io/hello-world:latest
Volume=foo.volume:/foo
EOF

❯ sudo tee /etc/containers/systemd/foo.volume > /dev/null << EOF
[Volume]
User=1000                         
EOF                   

❯ sudo systemctl daemon-reload

❯ sudo systemctl start foo

❯ sudo ls -ld /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-foo/_data
drwxr-xr-x 2 matthijs root 4096 Jan  5 18:27 /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-foo/_data

❯ sudo podman volume rm systemd-foo
systemd-foo

❯ sudo systemctl start foo

❯ sudo ls -ld /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-foo/_data
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Jan  5 18:27 /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-foo/_data
```


### Describe the results you received

The volume is *not* recreated by the foo-volume.service with the right options (uid=1000). Instead, the container is started and podman implicitly creates the volume with the default options (uid=0).

This is also visible in the journal, the first time `foo-volume.service` is started, but the second time it is not:

```
❯ sudo journalctl -u foo.service -u foo-volume.service -e
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo systemd[1]: Starting foo-volume.service...
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo podman[55776]: 2026-01-05 18:27:14.331612086 +0100 CET m=+0.023455833 volume create systemd-foo
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo foo-volume[55776]: systemd-foo
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo systemd[1]: Finished foo-volume.service.
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo systemd[1]: Starting foo.service...
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo podman[55792]: 2026-01-05 18:27:14.370545545 +0100 CET m=+0.021431909 container create 59254861fda8aa05f5eaa939459f7ff4ae8d410b5fd9804702cd07fb8807de1f (image=docker.io/library/h>
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo podman[55792]: 2026-01-05 18:27:14.357800554 +0100 CET m=+0.008686935 image pull 1b44b5a3e06a9aae883e7bf25e45c100be0bb81a0e01b32de604f3ac44711634 docker.io/hello-world:latest
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo podman[55792]: 2026-01-05 18:27:14.46780586 +0100 CET m=+0.118692244 container init 59254861fda8aa05f5eaa939459f7ff4ae8d410b5fd9804702cd07fb8807de1f (image=docker.io/library/hell>
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo podman[55792]: 2026-01-05 18:27:14.473270748 +0100 CET m=+0.124157116 container start 59254861fda8aa05f5eaa939459f7ff4ae8d410b5fd9804702cd07fb8807de1f (image=docker.io/library/he>
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo systemd-foo[55914]: Hello from Docker!
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo systemd[1]: Started foo.service.
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo foo[55792]: 59254861fda8aa05f5eaa939459f7ff4ae8d410b5fd9804702cd07fb8807de1f
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo podman[55945]: 2026-01-05 18:27:14.494566818 +0100 CET m=+0.013907817 container died 59254861fda8aa05f5eaa939459f7ff4ae8d410b5fd9804702cd07fb8807de1f (image=docker.io/library/hel>
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo podman[55945]: 2026-01-05 18:27:14.892265994 +0100 CET m=+0.411607130 container remove 59254861fda8aa05f5eaa939459f7ff4ae8d410b5fd9804702cd07fb8807de1f (image=docker.io/library/h>
Jan 05 18:27:14 zozo systemd[1]: foo.service: Deactivated successfully.


Jan 05 18:27:28 zozo systemd[1]: Starting foo.service...
Jan 05 18:27:29 zozo podman[56286]: 2026-01-05 18:27:29.00155174 +0100 CET m=+0.026409568 volume create systemd-foo
Jan 05 18:27:29 zozo podman[56286]: 2026-01-05 18:27:29.010531364 +0100 CET m=+0.035389202 container create bed42283a7129114229d67cc03eb7a454328de82362c0c7db83ac3f1ead13552 (image=docker.io/library/h>
Jan 05 18:27:29 zozo podman[56286]: 2026-01-05 18:27:28.984573286 +0100 CET m=+0.009431165 image pull 1b44b5a3e06a9aae883e7bf25e45c100be0bb81a0e01b32de604f3ac44711634 docker.io/hello-world:latest
Jan 05 18:27:29 zozo podman[56286]: 2026-01-05 18:27:29.111775103 +0100 CET m=+0.136632946 container init bed42283a7129114229d67cc03eb7a454328de82362c0c7db83ac3f1ead13552 (image=docker.io/library/hel>
Jan 05 18:27:29 zozo podman[56286]: 2026-01-05 18:27:29.115609442 +0100 CET m=+0.140467272 container start bed42283a7129114229d67cc03eb7a454328de82362c0c7db83ac3f1ead13552 (image=docker.io/library/he>
Jan 05 18:27:29 zozo systemd[1]: Started foo.service.
Jan 05 18:27:29 zozo systemd-foo[56407]: 
Jan 05 18:27:29 zozo systemd-foo[56407]: Hello from Docker!
Jan 05 18:27:29 zozo foo[56286]: bed42283a7129114229d67cc03eb7a454328de82362c0c7db83ac3f1ead13552
Jan 05 18:27:29 zozo podman[56437]: 2026-01-05 18:27:29.132423223 +0100 CET m=+0.013521511 container died bed42283a7129114229d67cc03eb7a454328de82362c0c7db83ac3f1ead13552 (image=docker.io/library/hel>
Jan 05 18:27:29 zozo podman[56437]: 2026-01-05 18:27:29.526874745 +0100 CET m=+0.407973013 container remove bed42283a7129114229d67cc03eb7a454328de82362c0c7db83ac3f1ead13552 (image=docker.io/library/h>
Jan 05 18:27:29 zozo systemd[1]: foo.service: Deactivated successfully.
```

### Describe the results you expected

I would expect the volume to be recreated with the original options (or, which was my original case, possibly with updated options if they were changed in the meanwhile).


### Suggested fix

The obvious fix for this issue would be to *not* set RemainAfterExit=yes for volume units. If I set that explicitly in the volume file, things behave as expected:

```
❯ sudo tee /etc/containers/systemd/foo.volume > /dev/null << EOF
[Volume]
User=1000
[Service]
RemainAfterExit=no
EOF

❯ sudo tee /etc/containers/systemd/foo.container > /dev/null << EOF
[Container]
Image=docker.io/hello-world:latest
Volume=foo.volume:/foo
EOF

❯ sudo systemctl daemon-reload

❯ sudo systemctl start foo

❯ sudo ls -ld /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-foo/_data
drwxr-xr-x 2 matthijs root 4096 Jan  5 18:40 /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-foo/_data

❯ sudo podman volume rm systemd-foo
systemd-foo

❯ sudo systemctl start foo

❯ sudo ls -ld /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-foo/_data
drwxr-xr-x 2 matthijs root 4096 Jan  5 18:40 /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/systemd-foo/_data
```

AFAICS that would involve changing `true` to `false` in this line: https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/ed5f4d6513d1736f93d5aaa5f7d415a5cc846c39/pkg/systemd/quadlet/quadlet.go#L1197

There might be unintended consequences - I just started using podman, so I cannot oversee the results of this change well :-)

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

No (but one point release older, and looking at the code this did not change in git since then).

comments.body:
What you described is by design. 

The assumption is that volumes should persist across restarts. So, stopping the service does not delete the volume. As you pointed, if users wish to delete the volume, they have to do so manually via `podman volume rm`.

Also, as you mentioned, the systemd service is defined as  `RemainAfterExit=Yes`. This to make the independent service not exit and to allow the dependency mechanism to work.

As a result of the two, when users manually delete the volume, they also need to manually stop the corresponding service. This to allow systemd to run it again when needed (which will create the volume correctly)

```
# First time starting the service
$ sudo systemctl start foo

# Manually deleting the volume
$ sudo podman volume rm systemd-foo
systemd-foo

# Manually stopping the volume's service
$ sudo systemctl stop foo-volume.service

# Since this container exists, you can just start the service, but in general, you should restart it.
$ sudo systemctl restart foo
```

> The assumption is that volumes should persist across restarts. So, stopping the service does not delete the volume. As you pointed, if users wish to delete the volume, they have to do so manually via podman volume rm.

Yes, that was clear to me (thinking on it, it might be more consistent if the volume would indeed be deleted on stop, but also utterly pointless, so this was never unclear to me).

> Also, as you mentioned, the systemd service is defined as RemainAfterExit=Yes. This to make the independent service not exit and to allow the dependency mechanism to work.

I would argue that this is *not* allowing the dependency mechanism to work. I would say that the dependency on `foo-volume.service` should *ensure* that the volume exists when starting `foo.service`, which is not what is currently happening.

Also, there is now an inconsistency with rebooting: If I `podman volume rm foo` and then restart `foo.service`, it does not get recreated (by the quadlet config/service), but if I `podman volume rm foo` and then restart my system, it *does* get recreated as expected.

But maybe you mean there is another reason why `RemainAfterExit=yes` is needed and the volume service is not allowed to exit?

The reason for `RemainAfterExit=yes` is the consistency of systemd, not of podman or any of its elements. From [systemd documentation](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.service.html?__goaway_challenge=meta-refresh&__goaway_id=3b92db95117fdbe51b60fef4a9076cc7&__goaway_referer=https://www.google.com/) on `oneshot` units:

> Behavior of oneshot is similar to exec; however, the service manager will consider the unit up after the main process exits. It will then start follow-up units. RemainAfterExit= is particularly useful for this type of service. Type=oneshot is the implied default if neither Type= nor ExecStart= are specified. Note that if this option is used without RemainAfterExit= the service will never enter "active" unit state, but will directly transition from "activating" to "deactivating" or "dead", since no process is configured that shall run continuously. In particular this means that after a service of this type ran (and which has RemainAfterExit= not set) it will not show up as started afterwards, but as dead.

I've done some more digging and it seems that both setting it or not doing so can create consistency issues. So, I'm not sure I want to change the behaviour.

Having said that, I'm looking into adding an option to instruct Podman not to create non-existing named volumes. This will at least cause the main service to fail instead of running incorrectly.

> I've done some more digging and it seems that both setting it or not doing so can create consistency issues. So, I'm not sure I want to change the behaviour.

Can you comment on what consistency issues these are? I cannot see them in the docs you posted.

In particular, AFAICS the volume services exist exclusively to ensure the volume exists when a depending container is started (since the volume unit does not do anything else, such as on stop). And the docs for `Type=oneshot` say:

>            •   Behavior of oneshot is similar to simple; however, the service manager will consider the unit up after the main process exits. It will then start follow-up units.

So that should satisfy the goals: After the volume is created, the container can be started.

The docs then continue with:

>   RemainAfterExit= is
               particularly useful for this type of service.  Type=oneshot is the implied default if neither Type= nor ExecStart= are specified. Note that if this option is used without
               RemainAfterExit= the service will never enter "active" unit state, but will directly transition from "activating" to "deactivating" or "dead", since no process is configured that shall
               run continuously. In particular this means that after a service of this type ran (and which has RemainAfterExit= not set) it will not show up as started afterwards, but as dead.

Which suggests that `RemainAfterExit` is useful to keep the service in the active state, which might be useful for actual services, or things that are not intended to be started a second time, but this does not seem needed to me in this case (and running it a second time is explicitly supported by adding `--ignore`).

> Having said that, I'm looking into adding an option to instruct Podman not to create non-existing named volumes. This will at least cause the main service to fail instead of running incorrectly.

This sounds like a great idea. It does not solve the problem entirely, but at least prevents things from silently breaking, which helps a *lot*.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

> This sounds like a great idea. It does not solve the problem entirely, but at least prevents things from silently breaking, which helps a lot.

@matthijskooijman just letting you know that this was added here: https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/27867 and will part of the next release. I know it does not address the root of the issue, but it is a start.

While investigating this behavior I initially considered opening a new issue, but then I found this one and thought it fits well, so I am adding my observations here.

I think there are actually **two related aspects** to the behavior described above.

1. The `.volume` unit remains `active (exited)` after the dependent `.container` unit stops.
2. Because of that, the `.volume` unit is **not executed again**, even if the underlying Podman volume was manually deleted.

Since `.volume` units behave like a **oneshot ensure operation**, systemd still considers the unit successful.

This leads to the following situation:

- a `.container` unit starts and the referenced `.volume` unit creates the volume correctly
- the `.container` unit is stopped again
- the `.volume` unit remains `active (exited)`
- the underlying Podman volume is deleted manually
- the `.container` unit is started again

At that point the `.volume` unit is not executed again, so Podman implicitly recreates the missing volume with default settings instead of the options and possible labels defined in the `.volume` file.

This means the configuration defined in the `.volume` quadlet is silently bypassed.

My assumption was that the built-in `--ignore` flag of `podman volume create` should already cover this situation: if the volume exists nothing happens, and if it does not exist it should be created again with the defined options.

However, due to the `.volume` unit remaining `active (exited)`, the creation step is never executed again.


@ygalblum You mentioned a fix  in this issue. Does that fix address exactly this behavior?
